---
title: "Theorem 3.2 — OLS normal equations and orthogonality of regressors and residuals"
lean_name: "HansenEconometrics.normal_equations"
chapter: 3
---

## Textbook statement

For a design matrix $X \in \mathbb{R}^{n\times k}$ and response vector
$y \in \mathbb{R}^n$, Hansen Theorem 3.2 states that when
[$X^\top X$ is invertible](../../assumptions/chapter3.qmd), the OLS estimator
$$\hat\beta = (X^\top X)^{-1} X^\top y$$
satisfies the **normal equations**
$$X^\top (y - X\hat\beta) = 0,$$
equivalently $X^\top \hat e = 0$ where $\hat e = y - X\hat\beta$ is the residual
vector. So the regressors are orthogonal to the OLS residuals. No
distributional assumption is needed; Theorem 3.2 is purely linear-algebraic.

### Proof sketch

Substitute the closed form $\hat\beta = (X^\top X)^{-1} X^\top y$ into
$X^\top (y - X\hat\beta)$:
$$X^\top y - X^\top X (X^\top X)^{-1} X^\top y = X^\top y - X^\top y = 0.$$
That's the whole proof: the normal equations are immediate from the definition
of $\hat\beta$.

Uniqueness (`olsBeta_eq_of_normal_equations`) goes the other direction: from
$X^\top(y - Xb) = 0$, multiply through by $(X^\top X)^{-1}$ to recover
$b = (X^\top X)^{-1} X^\top y = \hat\beta$.

## Lean statement

The corresponding Lean objects all live in
`HansenEconometrics/Chapter3LeastSquaresAlgebra.lean`:

- [`olsBeta`](https://github.com/apoorvalal/lean-hansen-econometrics/blob/main/HansenEconometrics/Chapter3LeastSquaresAlgebra.lean#L20)
  (definition, line 20) — the closed-form OLS coefficient
  $(X^\top X)^{-1} X^\top y$, defined under `[Invertible (Xᵀ * X)]`.
- [`fitted`](https://github.com/apoorvalal/lean-hansen-econometrics/blob/main/HansenEconometrics/Chapter3LeastSquaresAlgebra.lean#L38)
  (definition, line 38) — the fitted vector $X\hat\beta$.
- [`residual`](https://github.com/apoorvalal/lean-hansen-econometrics/blob/main/HansenEconometrics/Chapter3LeastSquaresAlgebra.lean#L42)
  (definition, line 42) — the residual vector $y - X\hat\beta$.
- [`normal_equations`](https://github.com/apoorvalal/lean-hansen-econometrics/blob/main/HansenEconometrics/Chapter3LeastSquaresAlgebra.lean#L46)
  (theorem, **line 46**) — the headline result:
  `Xᵀ *ᵥ residual X y = 0`.
- [`regressors_orthogonal_to_residual`](https://github.com/apoorvalal/lean-hansen-econometrics/blob/main/HansenEconometrics/Chapter3LeastSquaresAlgebra.lean#L61)
  (theorem, line 61) — same statement, packaged under the orthogonality reading.
- [`olsBeta_eq_of_normal_equations`](https://github.com/apoorvalal/lean-hansen-econometrics/blob/main/HansenEconometrics/Chapter3LeastSquaresAlgebra.lean#L67)
  (theorem, line 67) — uniqueness: any `b` satisfying the normal equations
  equals `olsBeta`.

The headline Lean theorem reads:

::: {.lean-hover-panel}
```{=html}
<pre><code><span class="lean-token" tabindex="0" data-explain="The citeable theorem name for Hansen Theorem 3.2's normal-equations face.">theorem normal_equations</span>
    <span class="lean-token" tabindex="0" data-explain="The fixed design matrix. Lean indexes rows by n and columns by k instead of using natural-number dimensions directly.">(X : Matrix n k ℝ)</span> <span class="lean-token" tabindex="0" data-explain="The response vector, represented as a function from row indices to real values.">(y : n → ℝ)</span> <span class="lean-token" tabindex="0" data-explain="Hansen's full-column-rank hypothesis, packaged as an instance so the closed-form inverse elaborates.">[Invertible (Xᵀ * X)]</span> :
    <span class="lean-token" tabindex="0" data-explain="Premultiply the OLS residual by X transpose: this is X' e-hat in textbook notation.">Xᵀ *ᵥ residual X y</span> = <span class="lean-token" tabindex="0" data-explain="The zero vector in the regressor-coordinate space. This says every regressor column is orthogonal to the residual.">0</span>
</code></pre>
```
:::

### Reading the statement

In English: **given** a design matrix $X$, a response vector $y$, and a witness
that $X^\top X$ is invertible, the OLS residual is orthogonal to every column
of $X$.

| Lean | Math | What it gives us |
|---|---|---|
| `(X : Matrix n k ℝ)` | $X \in \mathbb{R}^{n\times k}$ | **Given:** design matrix. The index types `n` (observations) and `k` (regressors) are arbitrary `Fintype`s, supplied by the section variables. |
| `(y : n → ℝ)` | $y \in \mathbb{R}^n$ | **Given:** response vector. Lean models a real vector as a function `n → ℝ` from the index type to reals. |
| `[Invertible (Xᵀ * X)]` | $X^\top X$ invertible | **Given (hypothesis):** the Gram matrix is invertible — Hansen's full-column-rank assumption. See the [Chapter 3 assumption map](../../assumptions/chapter3.qmd). The square brackets mark this as an instance argument: it is supplied by typeclass resolution rather than as an explicit `have`. |
| `Xᵀ *ᵥ residual X y = 0` | $X^\top \hat e = 0$ | **Conclusion:** matrix-times-vector applied to the residual equals the zero vector in $\mathbb{R}^k$. `Matrix.transpose` is written `Xᵀ`; `*ᵥ` is `Matrix.mulVec`. |

(The usual ambient typeclasses `[Fintype n] [Fintype k] [DecidableEq k]` are
hoisted into the file's `variable` block, which is why they don't appear in
the signature.)

## Translation notes

- Hansen writes $X' \hat e = 0$ in matrix-of-real-numbers notation. Lean's
  `Xᵀ *ᵥ residual X y = 0` is the same statement under Mathlib's
  `Matrix.mulVec` / transpose conventions.
- Lean parameterizes by typeclasses `[Fintype n] [Fintype k] [DecidableEq k]`
  and an `[Invertible (Xᵀ * X)]` instance instead of a side condition. This is
  the same hypothesis as Hansen's "$X^\top X$ is invertible," packaged for
  elaboration.
- The pair `olsBeta` (using `⅟`) and `olsBetaStar` (using `Matrix.nonsingInv`)
  coexist because Chapter 7's stochastic story needs the total version. They
  agree under invertibility
  ([`olsBetaStar_eq_olsBeta`](https://github.com/apoorvalal/lean-hansen-econometrics/blob/main/HansenEconometrics/Chapter3LeastSquaresAlgebra.lean#L31)
  at line 31).

## Tactic-by-tactic walkthrough

Hover over a proof line to read what it contributes.

::: {.lean-hover-panel}
```{=html}
<pre><code><span class="lean-line" tabindex="0" data-explain="The theorem statement: for any fixed design matrix X and response vector y, if X'X is invertible, the residual is orthogonal to the columns of X.">theorem normal_equations
    (X : Matrix n k ℝ) (y : n → ℝ) [Invertible (Xᵀ * X)] :
    Xᵀ *ᵥ residual X y = 0 := by</span><span class="lean-line" tabindex="0" data-explain="Replace residual, fitted, and olsBeta by their definitions. The goal becomes the textbook substitution X'(y - X (X'X)⁻¹ X'y) = 0.">  unfold residual fitted olsBeta</span><span class="lean-line" tabindex="0" data-explain="Distribute the outer X' over subtraction, turning X'(y - fitted) into X'y minus X' fitted.">  rw [mulVec_sub]</span><span class="lean-line" tabindex="0" data-explain="Introduce a local helper that converts the nested matrix-vector action X'(X((X'X)⁻¹(X'y))) into one matrix product acting on X'y.">  have hmul : Xᵀ *ᵥ (X *ᵥ (⅟ (Xᵀ * X) *ᵥ (Xᵀ *ᵥ y)))
            = (Xᵀ * (X * ⅟ (Xᵀ * X))) *ᵥ (Xᵀ *ᵥ y) := by</span><span class="lean-line" tabindex="0" data-explain="Prove the helper by applying Matrix.mulVec_mulVec backwards twice, fusing adjacent matrix-vector multiplications into matrix multiplication.">    rw [← Matrix.mulVec_mulVec, ← Matrix.mulVec_mulVec]</span><span class="lean-line" tabindex="0" data-explain="Start a calculation chain from the distributed expression. Each following line rewrites the second term until the subtraction is v - v.">  calc</span><span class="lean-line" tabindex="0" data-explain="Apply hmul to rewrite the nested fitted-value term as a single matrix product multiplying X'y.">    Xᵀ *ᵥ y - Xᵀ *ᵥ (X *ᵥ (⅟ (Xᵀ * X) *ᵥ (Xᵀ *ᵥ y)))
        = Xᵀ *ᵥ y - (Xᵀ * (X * ⅟ (Xᵀ * X))) *ᵥ (Xᵀ *ᵥ y) := by rw [hmul]</span><span class="lean-line" tabindex="0" data-explain="Reassociate matrix multiplication so X'X sits directly next to its inverse. This exposes the cancellation.">    _ = Xᵀ *ᵥ y - (((Xᵀ * X) * ⅟ (Xᵀ * X)) *ᵥ (Xᵀ *ᵥ y)) := by rw [Matrix.mul_assoc]</span><span class="lean-line" tabindex="0" data-explain="Use invertibility to collapse (X'X)(X'X)⁻¹ to the identity matrix.">    _ = Xᵀ *ᵥ y - (1 *ᵥ (Xᵀ *ᵥ y)) := by rw [mul_invOf_self]</span><span class="lean-line" tabindex="0" data-explain="Simplify identity-times-vector and v - v. This is the final textbook cancellation X'y - X'y = 0.">    _ = 0 := by simp</span>
</code></pre>
```
:::

For uniqueness,
[`olsBeta_eq_of_normal_equations`](https://github.com/apoorvalal/lean-hansen-econometrics/blob/main/HansenEconometrics/Chapter3LeastSquaresAlgebra.lean#L67)
(line 67) takes the hypothesis $X^\top(y - Xb) = 0$, rewrites it as
$X^\top y = (X^\top X) b$, and multiplies both sides by $⅟(X^\top X)$ to
identify $b$ with `olsBeta X y`.

## Downstream uses

`normal_equations` is one of the most reused lemmas in the algebra of OLS.
Direct consumers in this repo include:

- [`residual_sum_zero_of_one_mem_colspan`](https://github.com/apoorvalal/lean-hansen-econometrics/blob/main/HansenEconometrics/Chapter3LeastSquaresAlgebra.lean#L98)
  (Chapter3LeastSquaresAlgebra.lean:98) — when $\mathbf{1} \in \mathrm{col}(X)$,
  residuals sum to zero (Hansen equation 3.17).
- [`regressors_transpose_mul_annihilator`](https://github.com/apoorvalal/lean-hansen-econometrics/blob/main/HansenEconometrics/Chapter3FWL.lean#L37)
  (Chapter3FWL.lean:37) — $X^\top M = 0$, used throughout FWL.
- [`fitted_dot_residual`](https://github.com/apoorvalal/lean-hansen-econometrics/blob/main/HansenEconometrics/Chapter3Projections.lean#L217)
  (Chapter3Projections.lean:217) — fitted values are orthogonal to residuals.
- The Frisch–Waugh–Lovell theorems of Hansen Theorem 3.5 consume this
  transitively through the annihilator identity.
